experiences.intentions:
Player 4: No role-specific knowledge as a Servant on round 1; team [0,3] excludes me and gives little information. As Good in early game, approving a 2-person first quest is standard unless there is suspicious context, and approving avoids unnecessary early rejection while allowing information from quest outcome. My vote reveals little beyond normal cooperative play.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are evil, so 3 is the safest unknown good candidate besides myself. The current phase is team voting on quest 1, and approving a team of [0,3] is optimal because it excludes known evil players and gives good the best chance at an early success. My vote supports a clean team and reveals little beyond preferring an evil-free lineup, which is acceptable.
Player 1: I know Player 2 is my evil teammate, while 0 and 3 are both good from my perspective. On team voting in round 1, an all-good team [0,3] is dangerous for evil because it can succeed and give good early momentum. As evil, optimal play is usually to reject teams that exclude all evil players, especially early when little information is revealed. My reject gives limited info because early-round rejects are common and not strongly alignment-indicative.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0 and 3 on this proposed team are not confirmed evil from my perspective, so this team likely has no evil member and would succeed. In round 1 team voting, as evil, I generally want to avoid approving clean teams that give good an early success. Rejecting also keeps suspicion diffuse this early. My vote reveals limited information since round-1 rejections are common, and rejecting helps evil more than approving a likely-good team.
Player 3: As a Servant on round 1 with no prior info, I have no concrete reads on roles yet. The proposed 2-person team includes me and the leader, which is a standard opening team and likely to generate information from the quest result. As Good, approving a normal-looking opening team is usually optimal; rejecting without evidence gives little value and risks unnecessary stalls. My vote reveals willingness to test a conventional team, which modestly helps Good gather info.

Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] includes known evil player 2, so from a good-aligned perspective this team is bad and should be rejected. Rejecting also avoids enabling an evil-controlled quest and does not reveal more than necessary, since a reject on a team with a suspicious member is natural.
Player 4: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,3], so both 0 and 3 are somewhat more likely good, though a single evil could have passed. New team [0,2,3] keeps the two successful members and adds only one unknown, which is a strong team for Quest 2. As a good Servant, approving teams that contain the only currently validated players is optimal. My vote reveals I favor evidence-based continuity, which helps good more than evil here.
Player 2: I am Minion with teammate 1, so 0/3/4 are Good. Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,3], confirming both were likely good already. Current proposed team [0,2,3] includes me and two good players. As evil, I generally want to get evil onto missions and avoid looking too suspicious. Approving a team with exactly one evil on Quest 2 is strong because I can choose to fail later if on the mission; rejecting could look odd since my teammate 1 is excluded and this is an actually plausible good team. My vote reveals limited info and approval helps evil by sending me on quest.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 succeeded on team [0,3], so 0 and 3 look somewhat good but that team gave no evil info. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [0,2,3]; as evil, approving a team with one evil on quest 2 is strong because 2 can choose whether to fail and create ambiguity. My vote to approve is consistent with supporting my own team and helps get evil onto the quest without revealing extra information beyond expected leader behavior.
Player 3: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,3], which makes 0 and 3 somewhat more trustworthy though not confirmed because evil can pass early. Player 2 is less known. As a good Servant, approving a team containing myself and one prior-success member is reasonable; rejecting gives little benefit absent stronger suspicion. My vote reveals mild trust in 0 and willingness to test 2, which is acceptable for good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: Quest 1 with [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look somewhat good. Quest 2 with [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, which points to player 2 as the most likely evil, since 0 and 3 were on the clean first quest. As a good Servant on vote phase, I should reject teams that include the main suspect and exclude one or both cleaner players. Approving [0,2] risks sending likely evil 2 on quest 3 and gives little new info compared with safer alternatives.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 with team [0,3] succeeded, so at least one/both of 0 and 3 could be good; Quest 2 with [0,2,3] failed because I was on it and likely submitted fail, which puts suspicion on 0/2/3. Now in team voting on [0,2], as evil I want a small team containing me to go through because I can guarantee a fail and this also may frame 0 further. Approving helps evil more than rejecting; my vote itself reveals little because mixed voting is common.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know player 2 is evil. The proposed team [0,2] definitely contains evil, and on a 2-player quest that gives evil a strong chance to fail again. From prior results, quest 2 with [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, consistent with 2 being evil; quest 1 [0,3] succeeded, making 3 look good. As good, I should reject any team containing a known evil. Rejecting also avoids signaling too much beyond standard good play, since this team is obviously suspicious after the fail.
Player 1: I am Assassin with teammate 2. Proposed team [0,2] includes my evil partner and excludes me. Quest 2 with team [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, which could implicate 2 or me; since I was on that team and know 2 is evil too, approving [0,2] gives evil a strong chance to score another fail while also making 0 look suspicious. As evil, optimal play is to approve teams containing 2. My vote reveals little because evil may support this plausible team after 0 was on both prior quests.
Player 3: I’m Good Servant. Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and/or 3 look somewhat better but not confirmed. Quest 2 team [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, so exactly one of {0,2,3} is Evil. Since I know I am Good, that means exactly one of {0,2} is Evil if quest 1’s success is informative about 0/3; more strongly, 3 was on both a success and the failed quest and cannot fail as Good, so suspicion is concentrated on 0 or 2. Proposed team [0,2] consists of the two most likely suspects and excludes a known Good me. As Good in team voting, I should reject teams that are likely to contain Evil, especially on quest 3 where success matters. Rejecting also signals that this pairing is highly suspicious and preserves information for better team construction next round.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are evil. Proposed team [0,3] excludes both known evils and matches the only successful 2-person team composition from prior results. On team voting for quest 3, approving this team is optimal for Good and reveals little beyond supporting a clean team.
Player 3: We know team [0,3] previously succeeded with 0 fails, while [0,2,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, strongly implicating player 2 if quest data is truthful. As a Good Servant in team voting on quest 3, the optimal play is to approve a team that excludes the strongest evil suspect and includes a previously successful pair. My vote reveals I support a consistency-based good team; approving helps Good more than rejecting, since this is a solid 2-player team and only round 2.
Player 4: Quest 1 with [0,3] succeeded, but Quest 2 with [0,2,3] failed, so at least one of 0 or 2 is evil while 3 looks somewhat better. As a Good Servant, I should avoid approving teams that include both uncertain players when a safer alternative exists. Proposed team [0,3] removes 2, which is better than the failed team and is consistent with 0/3 being the successful pair from Quest 1. Approving keeps pressure on the more suspicious 2 and doesn't reveal much beyond supporting the strongest available evidence.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; the failed quest team [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, which could plausibly be from 2 or me if I were on it, so 0 and 3 may still be viewed as mixed by others. Current phase is team voting on [0,3], a 2-person team with no evil guaranteed onboard. As evil, the optimal play is to reject a likely all-good team that could give Good a second success and strong clears. My vote reveals little because evil generally prefer rejecting clean-looking teams, and one reject among five is not too informative.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, and since I was on it that implicates me but not publicly confirms anything; 0 and 3 may still be seen as good by others because only one evil was present. Current phase is team voting on [0,3] for Quest 3. As evil, I want to maximize chances of a failed quest while preserving ambiguity. This team excludes both evil players, so if approved it will almost certainly succeed, moving Good to match point. Rejecting is optimal to avoid a clean good success and to signal suspicion of a team containing only players from prior missions. My vote itself reveals little because rejecting after a fail-tainted history is defensible.

On quest 4 with team size 3 in 5-player Avalon, approving an all-good team is optimal and reveals little harmful information since rejecting a clean team could look suspicious and risk later worse teams. Approve to maximize chance of immediate success to 3 Good quests.
Player 4: Known info: Quest 1 team [0,2,3] had 1 fail, so at least one of 0/2/3 is evil. Quest 3 team [0,3] succeeded with 0 fails, which strongly suggests both 0 and 3 are good unless evil intentionally passed; with only one fail on Quest 1, that makes 2 the most suspicious. I am 4 and know I am good. Current phase is team voting on Quest 4 with proposed team [0,3,4]. For good, approving a team made of the two most trusted players plus myself is optimal. My vote signals trust in 0 and 3 based on quest history and avoids introducing suspicious 2 or unknown 1.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, so team [0,3,4] contains no known evil. Quest 2 failed on [0,2,3], where I was the only guaranteed evil, so the table likely suspects me somewhat while 0 and 3 gained cover from Quest 3 succeeding together. As evil on team voting for Quest 4, the optimal play is to reject a team that likely succeeds with 0 fails, especially since good is already at 2 successes and this quest would put them at match point. Rejecting also avoids lending credibility to a clean team; even if it passes without my vote, my reject is consistent with evil interests.
Player 1: I know player 2 is evil with me, and the only failed quest was the team [0,2,3], strongly implicating 2 from my perspective while making 0 and 3 look cleaner. The current team [0,3,4] excludes both evils, so it is very likely to succeed and move Good to 3 quest wins. As evil on quest 4, I should reject a clean-looking all-good team to try to force a different proposal that includes an evil player. My vote reveals some opposition to a plausible good team, but that is acceptable since evil needs to stop this quest from passing cleanly.
Player 3: I know I am Good. Quest 1 with team [0,2,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, so either 0 or 2 is Evil since I was on it and cannot fail. Quest 3 with team [0,3] succeeded, which strongly suggests 0 is Good, making 2 the most likely Evil. Player 4 added themselves to [0,3,4] for Quest 4; since [0,3] already succeeded, 4 is not confirmed but is a reasonable add while excluding likely-Evil 2. As Good in team voting, I should approve teams centered on cleared players and avoiding likely Evil. Approving reveals consistency with my reads and helps Good attempt a strong team.

experiences.intentions: Player 1: I am Assassin with teammate 2. Failed quest was team [0,2], so from good perspective 2 is suspicious and likely not Merlin's trusted pick. Subsequent successful teams were [0,3] and [0,3,4], making 0 and 3 look widely trusted, with 4 added on the last success. Merlin likely avoided including obvious evil 2 and may have steered toward good-heavy teams. Among 0,3,4, player 0 appears on all three quests including the failed one with 2, which a Merlin might still do if reading 0 as good and 2 as evil; but non-Merlin good could also do that. Player 3 was on two successful teams and may have been central to identifying safe comps. With limited data, the best assassination target is the player most likely to have correctly navigated around evil: 3.
